---
title: "Measuring Campaign Impact with CausalImpact"
description: "A practical walkthrough of Google's CausalImpact package — from data to causal claim."
date: 2026-06-11
categories: [practical, causal inference, R, time series]
image: thumbnail.png
---

## The setup

You run an email campaign on week 10. Sales data exists for 20 weeks. Did the campaign cause an increase in sales?

This is exactly the problem `CausalImpact` (Google, 2014) was built for. It fits a Bayesian structural time series model on the pre-intervention period, then extrapolates what *would have happened* without the intervention — your counterfactual.

---

## The data

```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(CausalImpact)
library(tidyverse)

set.seed(42)

# Simulate 20 weeks of sales
# Campaign starts on week 10
n <- 20
time <- 1:n

# Control series (unaffected by campaign)
control <- 100 + cumsum(rnorm(n, 0, 2))

# Treatment series: follows control, then bumps up after week 10
treatment <- control + rnorm(n, 0, 3)
treatment[10:n] <- treatment[10:n] + 15  # true effect = +15

df <- data.frame(
  week = time,
  sales = treatment,
  control = control
)

head(df)
```

---

## Running CausalImpact

The package needs:
1. A time series matrix (treatment + optional control series)
2. The pre/post period indices

```{r}
# Define periods
pre_period  <- c(1, 9)
post_period <- c(10, 20)

# Format for CausalImpact (zoo object)
ts_data <- zoo(
  cbind(sales = df$sales, control = df$control),
  order.by = df$week
)

# Run the model
impact <- CausalImpact(ts_data, pre_period, post_period)
```

---

## Reading the results

```{r}
plot(impact)
```

The three panels show:

- **Original** — actual vs. predicted (counterfactual) series
- **Pointwise** — difference at each time point
- **Cumulative** — total accumulated effect

```{r}
summary(impact)
```

```{r}
summary(impact, "report")
```

---

## What the numbers mean

The key outputs:

| Metric | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Average** | Estimated effect per time point |
| **Cumulative** | Total effect over the post period |
| **p-value** | Probability of this effect under no intervention |

In our simulation, we injected a true effect of +15 units. CausalImpact should recover something close to that — the gap between what actually happened and what the model predicts would have happened.

---

## Key assumptions

CausalImpact is not magic. It relies on:

::: {.callout-warning}
### When this breaks down

1. **The control series must be unaffected by the treatment.** If your "control" was also exposed to the campaign, the counterfactual is contaminated.

2. **The relationship between treatment and control must be stable.** If they diverged before the intervention, the model's extrapolation is unreliable.

3. **No other events happen at the same time.** If a competitor launched something on week 10 too, you can't isolate your campaign's effect.
:::

---

## When to use this

CausalImpact is a good fit when:

- You have **high-frequency data** (daily/weekly)
- You have **no control group** but have related series
- The intervention has a **clear start date**
- You can argue the control series is a valid proxy for the counterfactual

Classic applications: measuring the effect of a product launch, a PR event, a policy change, or an algorithm update.

---

## Next steps

- Try with your own data — any before/after situation works
- Experiment with adding multiple control series
- Compare with a manual difference-in-differences approach on the same data

Code for this post is [on GitHub](https://github.com/yourusername).

---

## References

- Brodersen et al. (2015). Inferring causal impact using Bayesian structural time-series models. *Annals of Applied Statistics*.
- [CausalImpact R package](https://google.github.io/CausalImpact/)
